title: Build Games Like a Solo Studio
summary: A public-safe reference system for solo devs and small teams using agents, practical production habits, and clear demo-to-release thinking.

landing.title: Build Games Like a Solo Studio
landing.summary: A clear starting point for vibe coders and solo studio builders who need structure, not jargon.

sections.title: Production and Scope
sections.summary: How to shape a vertical slice, control scope, and keep a game moving toward a real release.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When the studio changes its browser-first or demo-gate rules.

promptCards.title: Tighten my scope
promptCards.body: Ask your agent to cut a vague game idea down into one vertical slice with a visible review gate.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a game production director. Turn this game idea into one vertical slice with a clear title/menu flow, one complete playable loop, one art direction sentence, one audio direction sentence, a review gate, and a list of things that are explicitly out of scope for the first demo.

sections.title: Demo Signoff and Desktop Migration
sections.summary: How to separate browser-review health, honest demo readiness, and the moment it makes sense to move to a desktop production path.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When demo signoff language or migration defaults change.

promptCards.title: Should I stay on the web?
promptCards.body: Ask an agent to score the current slice honestly and tell you whether the browser is still helping or is now the bottleneck.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a studio production director. Evaluate this game using three separate labels: Browser Review Green, Demo Go, and Desktop Migration Triggered. Explain the current state honestly, define the smallest remaining gap, and say exactly when the team should stay in the browser versus move to the desktop production path.

sections.title: What A 2026 Demo Must Include
sections.summary: A clear bar for what separates a serious public-facing demo from a merely playable prototype in 2026.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When the studio changes its demo shortlist or demo-go criteria.

promptCards.title: Judge this demo honestly
promptCards.body: Ask an agent to score the build against a proper modern demo bar instead of just checking whether it runs.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a game director and demo producer. Evaluate this build against a serious 2026 demo bar. Judge title flow, onboarding, core loop, closure, readability, audio support, and overall public-facing clarity. Return: what already works, what is missing, the smallest set of fixes needed, and whether this is a real demo, a promising prototype, or still only a proof slice.

sections.title: Shared Demo Systems Across The Slate
sections.summary: The cross-game systems that should stay consistent even when the genres and platforms differ.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When the studio changes its shared demo discipline or manager workflow.

promptCards.title: Audit the shared demo systems
promptCards.body: Ask an agent which cross-game demo systems are already solid and which still need work.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a studio production lead. For this game, identify which shared demo systems are already in place, which are weak, and which are missing. Focus on entry flow, review route, onboarding, core loop, closure, audio, settings, and manager discipline. Return the smallest practical upgrade plan for reaching demo-ready discipline.

sections.title: Sound and Music
sections.summary: Audio direction for teams that need atmosphere, clarity, and practical milestone ladders without hiring a full audio department on day one.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When the studio changes its demo-ready audio bar or toolchain.

promptCards.title: Plan first-pass audio
promptCards.body: Get an agent to define what audio has to exist for the next reviewable build.
promptCards.prompt: Act as an audio director for a small game studio. Based on this game concept, write a first-pass audio plan covering menu mood, gameplay feedback, danger cues, music direction, placeholder rules, and the minimum audio pack required for a demo review.

sections.title: Graphics
sections.summary: Art direction, asset choices, and visual identity rules for small teams that need a strong look before they need huge content volume.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When the studio changes its art-pipeline defaults or engine mix.

promptCards.title: Define a visual lane
promptCards.body: Get a short art direction brief that makes your game immediately more specific.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a game art director. Turn this game idea into a practical visual direction brief covering palette, lighting, camera feel, asset density, environment mood, UI relationship, and the fastest way to avoid placeholder-looking scenes.

sections.title: Engines
sections.summary: How to choose engines and stacks without making the decision feel bigger than the game itself.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When major engine releases or platform constraints change the tradeoffs.

promptCards.title: Pick the engine
promptCards.body: Have an agent compare browser, Godot, Unity, and Unreal against your actual game constraints.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a technical game director. Compare the best engine or stack options for this game, including browser, Godot, Unity, and Unreal where relevant. Recommend one default path, one fallback path, the migration trigger, and the biggest technical risk for each option.

sections.title: Story
sections.summary: Narrative planning for teams that want flavor, hooks, and campaign texture without drowning in lore bloat.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When the studio changes its story-vs-systems balance in the core slate.

promptCards.title: Keep the story controllable
promptCards.body: Ask for a narrative scope that supports the loop instead of overpowering it.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a narrative designer for a small commercial game. Turn this concept into a story framework with one clear player fantasy, one clean opening hook, a light character/world cast, and the minimum narrative structure needed for the next demo without creating lore debt.

sections.title: Marketing
sections.summary: Discoverability, wishlist thinking, and public communication habits that should start before launch week.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When store algorithms, festival norms, or launch channels shift.

promptCards.title: Plan my first audience loop
promptCards.body: Ask an agent to turn the game into a repeatable message, clip plan, and wishlist loop.
promptCards.prompt: Act as an indie game marketing strategist. Based on this game, define the target audience, the one-sentence hook, three repeatable content angles, the best call to action for the current stage, and a low-overhead marketing loop a solo developer can actually maintain.

sections.title: Distribution
sections.summary: Where to ship, how to package, and when to treat stores, demos, and public builds differently.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When store policy, packaging requirements, or platform targets change.

promptCards.title: Choose release channels
promptCards.body: Ask an agent to turn your current build stage into a sane store and distribution plan.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a release operations producer. Based on this game and its current stage, recommend the right distribution channels, what to ship publicly now, what to keep private, the packaging expectations per channel, and the release risks that should be solved before a paid launch.

sections.title: UI/UX and Onboarding
sections.summary: Menus, tutorials, readability, and player orientation for projects that want to feel professional before they feel huge.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When the demo gate or controller/readability requirements change.

promptCards.title: Fix onboarding
promptCards.body: Ask for the smallest changes that make the first ten minutes clearer.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a game UX designer. Review this game concept and propose a title flow, first-run onboarding sequence, tutorial style, HUD readability rules, and settings menu essentials for a commercial-quality demo.

sections.title: QA, Playtesting, and Performance
sections.summary: How to check whether the game really works, not just whether the code runs on your machine.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When verification rules, capture tooling, or performance targets shift.

promptCards.title: Create a review plan
promptCards.body: Get a reusable checklist for a browser or desktop demo pass.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a game QA lead. Create a practical review checklist for this game covering launch path, menus, onboarding, one full loop, performance hotspots, UI clarity, sound behavior, and the evidence pack needed for a manager signoff.

sections.title: Launch and Store Readiness
sections.summary: The difference between a promising build and a product that is genuinely ready to face players and stores.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When store processes, trailer expectations, or launch checklists change.

promptCards.title: Tell me if this is launch-ready
promptCards.body: Ask an agent to score a build against real launch criteria instead of vibes.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a release producer. Evaluate this game against launch-readiness criteria covering content structure, save stability, menus, settings, onboarding, audiovisual finish, store assets, trailer readiness, screenshots, and packaging. Return a no-go, conditional, or go decision with the smallest set of fixes needed.

sections.title: Reviews and Criticism
sections.summary: How to write a game review, score a build honestly, and use a reviewer loop to sharpen demos before the public arrives.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When the internal reviewer rubric or review-process guidance changes.

promptCards.title: Review this build like a critic
promptCards.body: Ask an agent to review a game as a critic, not as a cheerleader, and return a score only if the evidence is strong enough.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a professional games critic. Review this build honestly using publication-style standards. Decide whether the right format is preview, demo review, review in progress, or full review. If the evidence supports a score, use a whole-number ten-point scale. Return: verdict, score or no-score decision, breakdown, strengths, weaknesses, who this is for, and the single highest-impact next fix.

sections.title: Agents, Handoffs, and Studio Workflow
sections.summary: How to work with AI agents as a solo studio without letting the workflow turn into chaos.
sections.nextReviewTrigger: When the studio changes its manager/session split or handoff model.

promptCards.title: Brief my agent
promptCards.body: Get a handoff prompt that tells an implementation agent exactly what success looks like.
promptCards.prompt: Act as a studio manager. Write a decision-complete implementation brief for an AI coding agent working on this game. Include product goal, current state, exact demo gate, constraints, acceptance criteria, evidence required, and what not to change.

promptPacks.title: Research Prompt Packs
promptPacks.summary: Reusable deep-research prompts for the categories in this reference system.
